results.possibleIdentity: Donald L. White was a prominent physicist and engineer at Bell Telephone Laboratories (Bell Labs) in Murray Hill, New Jersey, active from the late 1950s through the 1990s. His technical expertise spanned physics, optics, lasers, holography, and ultrasonics. In 1963, he received the IEEE Baker Prize for his paper 'The Depletion Layer Transducer,' which focused on ultrasonic devices. He was a pioneer in the field of optical and X-ray lithography, contributing to the development of soft X-ray projection lithography for semiconductor manufacturing and inventing techniques for mask-to-wafer alignment using Fresnel zone plates. Beyond his industrial research, White was deeply involved in the intersection of art and technology, frequently collaborating with artists. In 1977, he worked with German artist Dieter Jung to develop the technical requirements for 'Hologramm,' which is considered the first holographic poem. He also provided technical assistance to artist Betty Beaumont for her 'Ocean Landmark' project (1978–1980), an underwater environmental sculpture. He lived and worked in the vicinity of Mendham and Murray Hill, New Jersey.
